title: Apply: force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field
description: Apply your knowledge of the force experienced by a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field in this set of free practice questions.
topic_path: Apply: force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current $(\color{#4a8cf4}{\vec{I}\,}\color{black}{)}$ flows through a conductor, which is perpendicular to an external magnetic field $(\gray{\vec{B}}).$ As a result, the conductor experiences a force.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Complete the statement.**

* If the amount of current increases, but the direction of current does not change, then the force experienced by the conductor $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Current flowing through a conductor is represented by  a vertical arrow pointing down, labelled "I". The current passes through a magnetic field which is represented by horizontal arrows pointing to the right, labelled "B".

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current $(\color{#4a8cf4}{\vec{I}\,}\color{black}{)}$ flows through a conductor, which is perpendicular to an external magnetic field $(\gray{\vec{B}}).$ As a result, the conductor experiences a force.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statement.**

* If the amount of current decreases, but the direction of current does not change, then the force experienced by the conductor $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Current flowing through a conductor is represented by  a vertical arrow pointing up, labelled "I". The current passes through a magnetic field which is represented by horizontal arrows pointing to the left, labelled "B".

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current $(\color{#4a8cf4}{\vec{I}\,}\color{black}{)}$ flows through a conductor, which is perpendicular to an external magnetic field $(\gray{\vec{B}}).$ As a result, the conductor experiences a force.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Complete the statement.**

* If the direction of current reverses, but the amount of current does not change, then the force experienced by the conductor $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Current flowing through a conductor is represented by  a vertical arrow pointing up, labelled "I". The current passes through a magnetic field which is represented by horizontal arrows pointing to the left, labelled "B".

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current $(\color{#4a8cf4}{\vec{I}\,}\color{black}{)}$ flows through a conductor, which is perpendicular to an external magnetic field $(\gray{\vec{B}}).$ As a result, the conductor experiences a force.

[[☃ image 3]]

**Complete the statement.**

* If the direction of current reverses, but the amount of current does not change, then the force experienced by the conductor $\_\_\_\_\_\_.$

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Current flowing through a conductor is represented by  a vertical arrow pointing down, labelled "I". The current passes through a magnetic field which is represented by horizontal arrows pointing to the right, labelled "B".

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current $(\color{#4a8cf4}{\vec{I}\,}\color{black}{)}$ flows through a conductor, which is located in an external magnetic field $(\gray{\vec{B}}).$ The conductor is placed in three different orientations relative to the magnetic field as shown below. The amount of current is the same in each case.

[[☃ image 1]]

**In which orientation does the conductor experience the greatest force?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The force on a current-carrying conductor is greater when the *angle* between the current and magnetic field is greater.

In orientation $1,$ the current flow and magnetic field are $90\degree$ apart. They are *perpendicular*.

In orientation $2,$ the current flow and magnetic field are less than $90\degree$ apart.

In orientation $3,$ the current flow and magnetic field are $0\degree$ apart. They are *parallel*.
So, the conductor experiences the strongest force in orientation $1.$ The conductor experiences a weaker force in orientation $2,$ and zero force in orientation $3.$
Therefore, the conductor experiences the greatest force in orientation $1.$

options.alt: Horizontal magnetic field lines pointing to the right. A current-carrying wire is shown at three different angles relative to the magnetic field. In orientation 1, the current flows straight down. It's perpendicular to the magnetic field. In orientation 2, the current flows diagonally across the magnetic field. In orientation 3, the current flows to the right. It's parallel to the magnetic field.

choices.content: The conductor experiences the same amount of force in each orientation.

assessment_items.question_content:
An electric current $(\color{#4a8cf4}{\vec{I}\,}\color{black}{)}$ flows through a conductor, which is located in an external magnetic field $(\gray{\vec{B}}).$ The conductor is placed in three different orientations relative to the magnetic field as shown below. The amount of current is the same in each case.

[[☃ image 1]]

**In which orientation does the conductor experience the least force?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The force on a current-carrying conductor is greater when the *angle* between the current and magnetic field is greater.

In orientation $1,$ the current flow and magnetic field are $90\degree$ apart. They are *perpendicular*.

In orientation $2,$ the current flow and magnetic field are less than $90\degree$ apart.

In orientation $3,$ the current flow and magnetic field are $0\degree$ apart. They are *parallel*.
So, the conductor experiences the least (zero) force in orientation $3.$ The conductor experiences the strongest force in orientation $1,$ and a weaker (but nonzero) force in orientation $2.$
Therefore, the conductor experiences the least force in orientation $3.$

options.alt: Horizontal magnetic field lines pointing to the right. A current-carrying wire is shown at three different angles relative to the magnetic field. In orientation 1, the current flows straight down. It's perpendicular to the magnetic field. In orientation 2, the current flows diagonally across the magnetic field. In orientation 3, the current flows to the right. It's parallel to the magnetic field.

choices.content: The conductor experiences the same amount of force in each orientation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A **loudspeaker** (or **speaker**, for short) converts an electrical signal into sound. 

[[☃ image 1]]

A speaker's cone is attached to a conducting coil. The coil is in the field of a permanent magnet. When current flows through the coil, the coil experiences a force from the magnet. This force moves the coil and cone.

[[☃ image 2]]

 The moving cone produces sound waves in the air. The type of sound produced depends on how the cone is moving.

**Complete the explanation.**

* The sound's loudness is related to how *far* the cone moves. This is determined by the [[☃ dropdown 1]] of the force experienced by the coil. Therefore, loudness is controlled by the [[☃ dropdown 2]] of current through the coil.

* The sound's pitch is related to how frequently the cone moves *back and forth*. This is determined by how often the [[☃ dropdown 3]] of the force experienced by the coil changes. Therefore, pitch is controlled by how often the [[☃ dropdown 4]] of current through the coil changes.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: Loudness is related to how *far* the cone moves in the air. How far the cone moves is determined by the *magnitude* of force the coil experiences in the permanent magnet's field.
The magnitude of force experienced by the coil is determined by the *amount* of current flowing through it.
Pitch is related to how frequently the cone moves *back and forth*. The direction the cone moves is determined by the *direction* of force the coil experiences in the permanent magnet's field.

The direction of force experienced by the coil is determined by the *direction* of current flowing through it. Whenever the current's direction reverses, the direction of the force the coil experiences from the magnet reverses.

So:

The sound's loudness is related to how *far* the cone moves. This is determined by the magnitude of the force experienced by the coil. Therefore, loudness is controlled by the amount of current through the coil.

The sound's pitch is related to how frequently the cone moves *back and forth*. This is determined by how often the direction of the force experienced by the coil changes. Therefore, pitch is controlled by how often the direction of current through the coil changes.

options.alt: A black circle with a lightweight cone inside it. The cone points into the device, so the outside surface is pressed in like a crater.
options.caption: *External view of a speaker*

options.alt: The side view shows a copper coil connected to the inside of the cone. The coil is surrounded by a permanent magnet.
options.caption: *Internal diagram of a speaker*

assessment_items.question_content:
A **railgun** uses electricity to accelerate a projectile along two rails. 

As current flows through the rails, a magnetic field is produced around them. Current also flows through the projectile, which is located in the rails' magnetic field. So, the projectile experiences a force.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Analyze the model to complete the explanation.**

* In the space between the rails, the magnetic field from both rails is [[☃ dropdown 3]] the current through the projectile. This orientation results in [[☃ dropdown 5]] force on the projectile.

* Additionally, the amount of force experienced by the projectile is [[☃ dropdown 4]] related to the amount of current flowing through the rail gun. So, a larger current through the railgun results in a [[☃ dropdown 7]] projectile.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
The current flowing through the two rails produces a circular magnetic field around them. We can use the **right hand thumb rule** to confirm what's shown on the model: the magnetic field in the space between the rails points *up*.

The current through the projectile flows *across* this space, from one rail to the other. So, the magnetic field and current through the projectile are *perpendicular* to each other.

The force that a current-carrying conductor experiences from a magnetic field is greatest when the field and current are perpendicular $(90\degree)$ to each other. So, the rail gun's orientation *maximizes* the force on the projectile.

In addition to the orientation, the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field also depends on the *amount of current through the conductor* and the *strength of the magnetic field*. 

Increasing the current through the railgun increases both of these quantities. So, the force on the projectile is *directly* related to the amount of current.
The greater the force on the projectile, the greater its acceleration will be and the *faster* it will be moving when it leaves the rail gun.

So:

In the space between the rails, the magnetic field from both rails is perpendicular to the current through the projectile. This orientation results in maximum force on the projectile.

Additionally, the amount of force experienced by the projectile is directly related to the amount of current flowing through the rail gun. So, a larger current through the railgun results in a faster-moving projectile.

options.alt: Model of a railgun shows two parallel rails. A projectile rests across the rails and is free to move along them. Current flows down one rail, through the projectile, and back up the other rail. A circular magnetic field is produced around the rails. These magnetic field lines point up in the space between the rails, where the projectile is located.
